articles.title: Scientists found a protein that drives brain aging — and how to stop it
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists identified a protein called FTL1 as a driver of brain aging in mice. In aging mice, higher FTL1 levels weakened connections between brain cells and were associated with memory decline. When researchers reduced FTL1, the brain showed recovery, including rebuilding lost connections. Memory performance improved after FTL1 reduction in the reported experiments.

articles.title: These overlooked brain cells may control fear and PTSD
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers report that astrocytes play active roles in fear memory rather than acting only as support cells. The study found astrocytes interact with neurons in real time during forming, recalling, and weakening fear responses. Manipulating astrocyte activity changed the strength of fear memories. The findings suggest astrocytes could become targets for new anxiety-related disorder treatments.

articles.title: When our minds wander to the body, it may affect mental health
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers studied how spontaneous mind-wandering relates to bodily sensations and mental health. Brain imaging and self-reports indicated that mind-wandering episodes often coincide with increased awareness of physical states. The study linked such episodes with higher anxiety and depressive symptoms in some participants. The results highlight a possible role for cognition-body perception interactions in mental-health disorders.

articles.title: A gene mutation may trap the brain in the wrong reality in schizophrenia patients
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified a gene mutation that may impair updating of reality-related beliefs in schizophrenia by disrupting flexible decision-making. In mice, the mutation caused persistence with outdated choices despite changing conditions. The work implicated a thalamus–prefrontal cortex pathway as the affected circuit. Reactivating this pathway restored normal behavior in the experiments described.

articles.title: Scientists discover Alzheimer’s hidden “death switch” in the brain
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists reported a toxic pairing of two proteins that triggers brain cell destruction and memory loss in Alzheimer’s disease models. They described this interaction as a hidden "death switch" mechanism. A new compound that disrupts the pairing slowed disease progression in mice and protected brain cells. The intervention also reduced amyloid buildup in the reported mouse experiments.

articles.title: Scientists find hidden brain cells helping deadly cancer grow
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists in Canada reported that certain brain cells can help glioblastoma grow by sending supportive signals to tumor cells. These cells were previously thought to only support healthy nerves. When the researchers blocked this communication, tumor growth slowed in lab models. The study highlights a potential vulnerability in glioblastoma linked to tumor–brain cell signaling.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden gut signals that could detect cancer early
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study reported that gut bacteria and metabolites may provide biomarkers for earlier detection of serious digestive diseases. Using AI, researchers found that biomarkers linked to one condition can often predict others. The findings suggest these diseases may be more interconnected than previously thought. The authors propose that such signals could support faster diagnosis with fewer invasive procedures.

articles.title: Scientists create “smart” DNA drug that targets cancer cells with extreme precision
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists developed a programmable drug system built from synthetic DNA that targets cancer cells by sensing combinations of tumor markers. The system is designed to activate only when the specified marker pattern is detected, aiming to limit harm to healthy tissue. Researchers report it can be configured to deliver multiple drugs at once. The work is presented as a step toward responsive, machine-like medicines operating inside the body.

articles.title: What’s hiding inside colon cancer could change treatment
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers reported that colorectal tumors may have a distinct microbial "fingerprint" compared with other cancers. The study analyzed DNA data from more than 9,000 patients. It found that only colorectal tumors consistently hosted distinct microbial communities, challenging the idea that all cancers have unique microbial signatures. The findings are framed as potentially relevant to diagnosis and treatment development.

articles.title: Vaping Linked to Oral and Lung Cancers
articles.summary_4_sentences: An epidemiological analysis examined the relationship between e-cigarette use and cancer incidence. The results indicated a significant association between vaping and increased rates of oral and lung cancers. The study suggested that constituents of e-cigarette aerosols may contribute to carcinogenesis. The findings raise safety concerns about vaping products, particularly for younger populations.

articles.title: Scientists reveal new blood pressure treatment that works when others fail
articles.summary_4_sentences: A global trial reported that a pill called baxdrostat lowered blood pressure in people whose hypertension did not respond to standard treatments. Participants saw an average reduction of nearly 10 mmHg as described in the report. The article says the drug targets a hormone pathway involved in salt and water retention. The findings suggest a potential new option for uncontrolled hypertension.

articles.title: Scientists say BMI gets it wrong for over one third of adults
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study compared BMI-based classifications with body fat measurements from DXA scans. Researchers reported that more than one-third of adults were misclassified into incorrect weight categories by BMI. The mismatches included people labeled overweight or obese without corresponding body fat levels and others whose adiposity was missed. The results suggest BMI may be an unreliable proxy for body fatness for many adults.

articles.title: Hardly anyone uses this surprisingly simple blood pressure fix
articles.summary_4_sentences: A national analysis found that few Americans use salt substitutes despite their potential to reduce sodium intake and improve blood pressure. The study spanned nearly two decades and reported that usage remained low over time. Researchers characterized the low adoption as a missed opportunity for preventing heart disease and stroke. The article emphasizes that salt substitutes are a low-cost intervention that is underused.

articles.title: Cholesterol Drug Lowers Stroke Risk in High-Risk Patients
articles.summary_4_sentences: A randomized trial reported that evolocumab reduced the risk of first-time heart attack and stroke in high-risk patients. The study found fewer cardiovascular events in participants receiving evolocumab. Benefits were reported across subgroups, including people with prior statin intolerance. The findings support broader use of PCSK9 inhibitors for secondary prevention as described.

articles.title: This common vaccine cuts heart risk nearly in half in new study
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study reported that a shingles vaccine was associated with reduced cardiac events in high-risk people. The article states major cardiac events fell by 46% and deaths by 66% within a year in the studied group. It suggests preventing shingles may reduce clot-related complications that can lead to heart attacks and strokes. The reported effect size was compared in the article to the benefit of quitting smoking.

articles.title: Scientists discover why flu and COVID hit older adults so hard
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study reported that aging lungs can drive more dangerous outcomes from flu and COVID-19. Researchers identified lung cells that can trigger an exaggerated immune response, producing inflammatory cell clusters that damage tissue. Activating the aging-related signal in young mice made their lungs behave like older lungs and led to severe illness. The findings point to age-linked immune signaling in the lung as a contributor to severe disease.

articles.title: Are Autoantibodies to Blame for Long COVID?
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reviews evidence that autoantibodies may contribute to long COVID symptoms. A cohort of long COVID patients showed elevated antibodies against self-antigens. Higher autoantibody titers were correlated with fatigue and neurological complaints. The authors propose autoimmunity as a contributing mechanism in long COVID pathology.

articles.title: New "super antibiotic" stops deadly gut infection without destroying the microbiome
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers reported that a new antibiotic, EVG7, cleared Clostridioides difficile infection in mouse studies. The report says a small dose was effective and prevented recurrence in the model. Unlike many antibiotics, EVG7 appeared to spare beneficial gut bacteria that help suppress C. difficile. The findings suggest EVG7 could be a microbiome-preserving approach to treating this infection.

articles.title: This 5-day diet helped Crohn’s patients feel better fast
articles.summary_4_sentences: A clinical trial tested a fasting-mimicking diet consisting of five days per month of very low-calorie, plant-based meals in people with Crohn’s disease. Most participants reported noticeable symptom improvements during the study period. The diet was also associated with reduced biological markers of inflammation as described. The results suggest diet could be a meaningful adjunct in Crohn’s management based on the trial findings.

articles.title: Binge drinking just once a month may triple your risk of liver scarring
articles.summary_4_sentences: A large U.S. study examined heavy drinking frequency in people with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD). The study reported that binge drinking as infrequently as once per month was linked to a much higher risk of serious liver scarring in this group. MASLD was described as affecting about one in three adults. The results challenge the assumption that occasional binge drinking is harmless among otherwise moderate drinkers.

articles.title: This simple Japanese eating habit could help you live longer without dieting
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article describes hara hachi bu, a practice of eating until about 80% full. It emphasizes slowing down and attending to hunger cues rather than strict dieting. It reports that research suggests the practice may reduce calorie intake, support healthier food choices, and help prevent long-term weight gain. The piece frames hara hachi bu as a potentially simple habit for improving health behaviors.

articles.title: Eating more meat may lower Alzheimer’s risk for some people
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study reported that the relationship between meat intake and cognitive decline may differ by genetics. Older adults carrying high-risk APOE variants who ate higher amounts of meat did not show expected cognitive decline in the report. Those with these gene variants and the highest meat intake were described as having slower cognitive decline and lower dementia risk. The findings were presented as challenging conventional dietary advice for Alzheimer’s risk in this subgroup.

articles.title: Deafness reversed: One injection restores hearing in just weeks
articles.summary_4_sentences: A small study tested a gene therapy designed for people born deaf by delivering a working copy of a hearing-related gene into the inner ear. The treatment was administered via a single injection. All ten patients in the study, from young children to adults, showed improved hearing. Some participants experienced gains within one month according to the report.

articles.title: Artificial saliva made from sugarcane protein protects teeth from acid and decay
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists developed an artificial saliva that uses a sugarcane protein to protect tooth enamel and fight bacteria. The key ingredient, CANECPI-5, binds directly to enamel and forms a shield against acids that contribute to tooth decay. Early tests reported stronger protection when combined with fluoride and xylitol. The work is presented as potentially beneficial for people with reduced saliva production after cancer treatment.

articles.title: Breakthrough could protect the vision cells that let you see faces and colors
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers screened more than 2,700 compounds in lab-grown human retinal models to find molecules that protect cone photoreceptors. Cone photoreceptors were described as cells needed for sharp vision and color vision. The study identified several molecules that shielded cones from degeneration in the model system. It also highlighted a protective mechanism involving casein kinase 1 as a potential treatment target.

articles.title: Scientists discover protein that triggers diabetic blindness
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified a protein called LRG1 as an early trigger of damage in diabetic retinopathy. The report says LRG1 constricts small retinal blood vessels and reduces oxygen supply. In mice, shutting down LRG1 prevented early damage from developing. The findings suggest a possible approach to protecting vision before symptoms occur.

articles.title: Simple therapies beat drugs for knee arthritis pain relief
articles.summary_4_sentences: An analysis of nearly 10,000 patients evaluated non-drug approaches for knee osteoarthritis. It found knee braces, hydrotherapy, and exercise significantly eased symptoms and improved mobility. The report emphasizes these options avoid risks associated with common medications. The findings suggest accessible, low-cost therapies could play a larger role in treatment.

articles.title: Scientists discover hormone that may stop chronic back pain at its source
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study reported that a bone hormone may reduce chronic back pain through an effect on pain-sensing nerves. Instead of acting only on bone strength, the hormone appeared to prevent nerves from growing into damaged spinal areas. In animal models, this was associated with stronger spinal tissue and reduced pain sensitivity. The work suggests a possible route to treating back pain by addressing underlying biology.

articles.title: Women over 50 lost 35% more weight with this surprising combo
articles.summary_4_sentences: A Mayo Clinic study reported outcomes for postmenopausal women using tirzepatide with or without menopausal hormone therapy. Women who used menopausal hormone therapy while taking tirzepatide lost about 35% more weight than those taking tirzepatide alone. The report suggests a potential synergy between hormones and obesity medications. The findings are presented as relevant to personalized approaches for cardiometabolic risk after menopause.

articles.title: Scientists discover sleep switch that builds muscle, burns fat, and boosts brainpower
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers mapped neural circuits that link deep sleep to growth hormone regulation. The report describes a feedback loop in which sleep increases growth hormone and growth hormone influences wakefulness control. Growth hormone was discussed as supporting muscle and bone strength, metabolism, and mental performance. The work connects sleep physiology with hormonal regulation in the brain.

articles.title: Vivid dreams may be the secret to deeper, more restful sleep
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers reported that immersive, vivid dreams can be associated with feeling more rested. Participants reported their deepest sleep after intense dream experiences even when brain activity was high. The findings suggest that perceived sleep depth does not always correspond to low brain activity periods. The study proposes that dreaming may contribute to restorative sleep experiences.

articles.title: Study finds dangerous lead levels in children’s clothing
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers tested children’s shirts from multiple retailers and reported that all samples exceeded U.S. safety limits for lead. The report notes that young children may be exposed because they sometimes chew on clothing. Brightly colored fabrics such as red and yellow were described as having particularly high levels, potentially related to dye-fixing chemicals. Simulations suggested that even brief mouthing could lead to unsafe lead exposure linked to neurodevelopmental harm.

articles.title: Common pesticide may more than double Parkinson’s disease risk
articles.summary_4_sentences: A UCLA Health study reported that long-term exposure to the pesticide chlorpyrifos was associated with increased Parkinson’s disease risk. People living in areas with sustained exposure were reported as having more than 2.5 times the likelihood of developing Parkinson’s disease. Lab experiments described in the report showed exposed animals developed movement problems and lost dopamine-producing neurons. The animals also showed toxic protein buildup similar to what is seen in Parkinson’s patients according to the summary.

articles.title: Most U.S. states are warming but not in the way you think
articles.summary_4_sentences: An analysis reported that warming trends across U.S. states are uneven depending on which temperature measures are examined. About half of states showed rising average temperatures, while many others showed changes in extremes such as hotter highs or warmer lows. The report described regional differences, including more extreme heat in the West and fewer cold extremes in the North. The findings suggest climate change effects can look different depending on location and metric.

articles.title: Quantum physics can confirm where someone is located
articles.summary_4_sentences: Physicists demonstrated a method using quantum entanglement to verify a person's location without revealing additional information. The technique uses quantum correlations to confirm presence within a defined area. Experiments reported reliable confirmation over distances of several kilometers in controlled settings. The work is framed as potentially useful for security and privacy-preserving authentication.

articles.title: Fossils reveal many complex animals existed before the Cambrian explosion
articles.summary_4_sentences: Paleontologists reported fossil evidence of complex multicellular animals existing before the Cambrian explosion. The fossils were described as having specialized tissues and structures thought to appear later. Radiometric dating placed these organisms at least 540 million years old. The findings challenge the idea of a sudden evolutionary "explosion" and support a more gradual emergence of complexity.
